essence: Internet/e-commerce businesses are mostly platforms: they win through network effects and scale - the biggest marketplace attracts the most buyers and sellers.
Two monetisation engines: take rate on transactions (GMV x take rate) and advertising/services on top - ads are the high-margin gold.
It's a CAC/LTV + retention game with thin transactional margins; profit comes from scale, repeat usage, and monetising data/ads.
Logistics and fulfilment are the hard, capital-heavy part of physical e-commerce - both the moat and the cost.
Winner-take-most: liquidity and habit create durable leaders, but cash-burn and competition are brutal on the way.

definition.oneLiner: Platforms and stores that sell goods, services or attention online - connecting buyers and sellers (or serving content) at internet scale.
definition.jobToBeDone: Let people discover, buy and receive what they want online - faster, cheaper, with more choice than offline - and let sellers reach demand.

valueChain.profitPools: Thin profit in the transaction itself (logistics eats it); the real profit pools are advertising, third-party marketplace take rate, and high-margin add-ons (ads, fulfilment-as-a-service, subscriptions like Prime).
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting from pure marketplaces -> logistics & quick commerce (speed as the battleground) -> retail-media (advertising) and AI-driven discovery, where the margin actually lives.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per order (take rate / margin on GMV) plus ad & service income per user
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: GMV x Take rate. Net revenue grows with order volume, basket size, take rate, and especially advertising/services layered on top.

costStructure.shape: Thin gross margins on goods, dominated by fulfilment/logistics and customer-acquisition spend; advertising and services are the high-margin layers that turn the model profitable at scale.
costStructure.leverage: Operating leverage comes with density and scale (logistics fixed costs spread over more orders) and from high-margin ads; sub-scale players bleed on delivery and CAC.

competitionAndMoats.concentration: Winner-take-most within categories/geographies: a few dominant horizontal players plus vertical/niche specialists; quick commerce consolidating fast.

structuralForces.regulation: Rising - FDI and marketplace rules (especially India), data protection, antitrust scrutiny of platform power, seller-protection and consumer rules.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Consumer-spend and funding sensitive: discretionary categories swing with the economy; cash-burn models depend on funding availability.
structuralForces.substitution: Offline retail, other platforms, D2C brands going direct, and social commerce; for ads, the whole digital-ad market competes.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Large brands/sellers gain power as they build their own channels; consumers have near-zero switching cost (multi-homing); gig/delivery labour and logistics partners matter.

aiPenetration.maturity: High - search, recommendations, pricing, logistics and ads are deeply ML-driven; generative AI is now reshaping discovery and customer service.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI lifts conversion (discovery/personalisation) and cuts cost (logistics, support); agentic shopping could disintermediate today's storefronts, shifting power to whoever owns the AI shopping layer.

ranges: 1P retail gross margin: thin (single digits to teens)
Contribution margin per order: positive only at scale/density
Many players pre-profit; path-to-profit via ads + scale
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Improve contribution margin per order (logistics, AOV)

keyTerms.GMV: Gross Merchandise Value - total value of goods sold on the platform.
keyTerms.Take rate: The platform's revenue as a % of GMV.
keyTerms.AOV: Average Order Value - average spend per order.
keyTerms.Contribution margin: Revenue per order minus the variable costs to serve it.
keyTerms.1P vs 3P: First-party (platform owns inventory) vs third-party (marketplace sellers).
keyTerms.Retail media: Advertising sold to brands on the platform - the high-margin profit engine.
keyTerms.Quick commerce: Ultra-fast (10-30 min) delivery via local dark stores.
keyTerms.Dark store: A retail-less mini-warehouse used purely for fast online fulfilment.
keyTerms.Multi-homing: Users/sellers using several platforms at once - weakens lock-in.

questionsToAskFirst: Is there contribution-margin-positive unit economics per order?
What's the take rate and the ads/retail-media mix?
Repeat rate and CAC/LTV - is demand sticky and efficient?
How heavy is logistics cost, and is there density?
Path to profit - scale, ads, or both?
rookieTraps: Celebrating GMV growth while losing money per order
Underrating retail-media as the real profit engine

whatsUniqueHere: E-commerce is a brutal paradox: enormous revenue (GMV) with razor-thin margins, because logistics and customer acquisition devour the transaction. The winners don't get rich selling goods - they get rich selling attention (ads/retail-media) and services on top of the traffic they aggregate. Scale and habit create winner-take-most positions, but the road there is paved with cash burn, and AI-driven agentic shopping now threatens to reshuffle who owns the customer.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: A fast-growing, fiercely competitive market: horizontal leaders (Amazon India, Flipkart/Walmart), Reliance's JioMart, a booming quick-commerce segment (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart), food delivery (Zomato, Swiggy), and a thriving D2C/social-commerce layer; ONDC is a government push to open the rails.
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Fintech & Payments - platform, network effects, CAC/LTV

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India e-commerce is led by Flipkart and Amazon with Reliance scaling fast; quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart) is the hottest, most-contested battleground; profitability is improving for some (Zomato profitable) as retail-media/ads scale; ONDC is reshaping the rails.
